prevalence.source: FEBRASGO/IMS menopause guidance; women's health surveys
prevalence.trend: stable; absolute numbers rising with aging

burden.dalyPerCapita: quality-of-life impact from vasomotor and urogenital symptoms

epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: 12 months of amenorrhea after age 45; FSH testing if diagnosis uncertain

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no screening; clinical diagnosis in primary care
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Moderate; symptom control depends on access to counseling and hormone therapy

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Clinical diagnosis; rule out pregnancy, thyroid disease, and other causes of amenorrhea
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Lifestyle measures, non-hormonal options, and menopausal hormone therapy when eligible; vaginal estrogen for urogenital symptoms
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Unequal access to gynecology care and persistent fear of hormone therapy
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: SUS offers basic gynecologic care and widely available non-hormonal therapies
